speaker: interviewer
text: Six. I'm interviewing Bertha Leverton in London, England. Bertha, what happened after Dovercourt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for me, it wasn't a good episode, what happened after Dovercourt. But on tape, one has to tell the truth. It didn't matter really that the people we came to-- it mattered to a lot of children if they were Jewish or not, especially to children who came from religious backgrounds, like myself. It was explained to us in Dovercourt by a representative of the chief rabbi that not all the children will find homes in Jewish homes, although I cannot understand why not. That was-- that's 50 years later on. It was a very big undertaking, the rescue of so many children. And it is to the credit of the churches, to individual people who came to the rescue, who took us in, Jewish and non-Jewish. We had-- I must make it clear now that we had wonderful places with Christian people and wonderful places with Jewish people, and mediocre or run-of-the-mill places with either, with religious-- either religion. And we had bad experiences with either religion as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad. My sister, with whom I argued very much about it, because tell her-- she wouldn't tell the truth in her story in my book. Because she said she doesn't like to tell bad stories. She sort of glossed it over. But one good thing, we were together. My brother went from the camp first. He was taken over by a family, a milkman and his wife, in Coventry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't have much choice. They did ask us, were you Orthodox at home? Do you want Jewish? Of course, we said we would like a Jewish family. But there weren't enough to go around. So he was taken into a, a non-Jewish home  in Coventry. And he was, at that time, 12. And what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked me if I would like to go to Coventry to be near my brother. And of course, I said yes. So they allocated me to a family called Sharpe, with an E at the end. And now I can appreciate that you don't become a slave overnight. It was gradual. It's a gradual process. They made me very welcome. I was, by that time, 16. I had my 16th birthday in the camp. And I remember how nice the-- we had sort of aunties to look after us-- how nice. And I still remember what I got for my birthday. I had a lovely handkerchief and a marzipan pig. And I had, I think, it was half a crown in English money. That was very nice. And I came to that family. And I didn't realize that they had wanted a maid. See, we weren't supposed to come as a labor force. We were supposed to be children. We were-- sorry, we were supposed to be children, to have an education. I am sadly lacking in an English education. I have never caught up with it. My spelling is atrocious. My style of writing is not bad, because it followed me from the German into English. I learned English very, very quickly, which was just one of those things as younger people do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much English did you have when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, almost nil, you know, just a sentence or two. She spoke-- it was a youngish family. She was only, actually, a few years younger than my-- a few years older than myself. She was fairly newly married to her husband, who had been married before. And he was a lot older than her. To me, he seemed ancient. He-- she was only seven years older than me, which made her 20-- what, 16 and--

words.text: a few years younger than my--

speaker: interviewee
text: 23-- 23, 24, that's all she was. And he was another 14 years older. I didn't mind the, the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all sorts of house-- you know, all the household things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everything. I mean, she was nice and slim when I came to her. And when I left five years later, she was as fat as a pig, because she never moved. I mean, I did everything. But by that time, I went out to work as well besides that. But in Coventry, we weren't allowed to do any work apart from working in the house. And people used to come every few months from the committee, and how are you, and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I had nice clothes. In fact, the trouble was my clothes were nicer than her. Because when your parents pack your suitcase, what do they pack? Not the rubbish-- they pack your best things. So I had plenty of nice clothes to start off with, which she pinched most of them. And so it went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- far worse than that-- I'd like to just read you one sentence in my story, which I think tells it all. I don't want to go into details. I just said, 'Looking back over the years, one normally recalls not the ordinary things that happen in one's life, but the happy or sad events. They may recede into the background but are never forgotten. The memory of five years I spent with my foster family can never be erased. On the bonus side, they also kept my younger brother, Theo, aged 12, and my sister Inge, aged 9. I was just 15 on my-- I was 15 on my arrival in England. The treatment of me by Auntie Vera'-- we had to call them aunt and uncle-- 'I can now put down to her resentment of my good health, she being a semi-invalid. But her torment of me, of us, and me in particular, was nothing compared to Uncle Billy's friendliness, which I successfully managed to avoid for five years.' So I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something more about that, Bertha?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really want to go into details. But it was very difficult for a 16-year-old up to the age of 21, when we had the great, great fortune to be reunited with my parents, whom we never told of that episode, to live under the roof of such-- in, in such a home. The thing was, she knew about it. And she wouldn't have cared tuppence if he had succeeded in what he didn't succeed in. And so that's all. It was pretty horrible. Later on, when Coventry was blitzed, we went through The Blitz, which was also an episode. I'm a natural-born worker. I like work. I don't like to be idle. But I would have loved to be able to go to a night school, which was denied me. And I don't know if anybody can visualize a child, you know from quite a, not a rich home, but sort of  a fairly educated home, who loves, adores, reading. My English was learned a-- from her. She spoke very good English-- and a magazine. The only thing, the only reading matter I ever saw in that house for five years was a magazine called or I don't know what it was called then. There wasn't a book in the house. I wasn't even allowed to join a library. And for me, that was torture. It was dreadful for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing you could do about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing. I was there. I-- see, the hold they had over me all those years were really my brother and sister. I could have got away later on when I became a bit older. I could have supported myself any time by the time I would have been allowed to work, especially after we evacuated from there to-- after the Coventry blitz, we evacuated to the-- Yorkshire, on the-- on the border of Lancashire and Yorkshire, to a little village called Delph. I could never get away from them. Because they said point-blank they would keep my sister. They would never have released her. And they had such a hold over her. She won a scholarship. She-- of course, she had to go to school. And my brother went to school for a short time till age 14. Then they sent him out to work as well. And they had like a Svengali hold over her. And they tried their best to split us. Actually, it was wonderful of them to give permission to bring her over. They had no children. When they saw her photograph, she was the most beautiful child. She's too good-looking. They wanted to adopt her. And of course, I wouldn't agree to that. And in the beginning, it was like I being the-- what  do you call that story-- Cinderella. And she was the princess. And she had-- she came with the prettiest clothes my parents sent her with. She came six months later. They took her out everywhere, to their friends and to relatives. And, and I was really the Cinders at home. But blood is thicker than water. They couldn't, they couldn't do that. And things got so bad that they, you know, that they mistreated her as well later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, It was more mental. For instance, when we lived in the-- when she'd won her scholarship, she had to travel by train every morning. And there was one train, a particular train, that could get her to school in Oldham, which was quite a few miles away from the village we lived. And just a few minutes before the train left, which was right down at the bottom of our road, Uncle Billy would say, oh, fetch me some cigarettes or fetch me something from the little shop. And the poor child was absolutely in, in, in terror in case she would miss her train and things like that. And you know, they'd deprive us. I mean, that was-- we were always hungry. That was another thing. We were really hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: They kept you short of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And specially, they took a great delight on our fast days. We had our Jewish calendar sent us. And we observed-- observed as much as we could. We couldn't observe the festivals. But we talked about it. And I taught the children, you know, to, probably to read a little bit of Hebrew and some of the prayers. That's one-- another thing I took with me, a siddur. That's a prayer book. It was most precious to us. And we couldn't observe or keep anything. But on the day, the day of Yom Kippur, when you fast in the evening, we were allowed our usual ration of two slices of bread. And we fasted the whole day after that. And next evening, we had the other two slices. But we didn't have anything that we'd missed out on, lunchtime. By that time, I was working in a cotton mill, which I enjoyed very much, I must say. It got me away from home, but things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we loved when they went to the pictures. Because then we would raid the larder a little bit, you know? We were really hungry. And, and Inge, she, she was sent shopping. And she used to snitch a few pence here and a few pence there. And she told me only a few months ago what she did.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- all the village-- that was after  the Coventry blitz when we evacuated, the whole village must have known about the state we, we, we were kept in. Because the neighbor, she used to ask for my sister when her husband went-- he was a traveler-- when he went away. And he-- she asked for my sister to sleep there with her. She pretended she didn't like to sleep alone. And the foster parents couldn't say anything. She-- they couldn't say no. And Inge loved going there to sleep. And she'd have a nice breakfast before she went to school. And she used to spoil her at night. And you know, and she used to bring things over for me sometimes. It was-- so that means the neighbors must have known. And especially when I worked in the cotton mill, they also knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to-- Inge, when she went shopping, she told me about a bottle of pop. She fancied a bottle of pop. So what did she do? She put it on the bill, which wasn't itemized.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was afraid to bring that bottle of pop home. She kept it in the shop. They kept it for her. And she'd go and have a drink from that bottle every time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's dreadful, you know? It, it seems funny now. But it wasn't funny at all. Or a packet of biscuits, which she kept there because she was afraid to bring it home. So we were kept very, very short.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm already jumping over the time when I was working in the cotton mill. Like I said, I loved it. Because-- and I was their, one of their top workers. We were on piecework, and, for that time, a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you do with the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ha, ha. What did I do with the money? In those days, three pound was a fortune. I really had top rates, brought it home. And from each pound, she was supposed to allow me half a crown, which was an eighth. And she'd give it me on the day. And next day or two days later, it was borrowed back again. And I never saw it again. I mean, I had no shoes. I wore clogs when my shoes wore out. That was the footwear, was clogs. When my parents came, I hadn't got a pair of shoes. I went to clogs-- with clogs to work and came home with them. And I-- there was one girl. She had a stepmother, sort of the wicked stepmother. And she used to-- she showed me how you alter the, the wage packet. And sometimes, we could only alter it by one pound. So sometimes if I had a very good week, say, four pound, she showed me how to rub it off and things like that. And we'd alter it to one pound. But it didn't help much. Because we were so terrified in case we'd be found with a pound. We gave that up after a while. We couldn't do anything with it. You can't go shopping, because she would know. You know, she kept the sweet rations of all of us. And we never had anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in fear all of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was terrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either of him or of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrified, yeah. It wasn't a life. I don't think, I don't think I would have grown up to be a normal person if my parents hadn't survived and, and rescued us. It was a most dreadful life. And once every six months, somebody came from the committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said everything was all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were there. They were there, you know? they warned us that-- and then we had to put our good clothes on, and whatever we had in good clothes. They used to ask for parcels. During the war, Americans sent over a lot of-- like the Red Cross. And they'd go and pick clothes for me. And the best ones were for her. And I had the-- you know, whatever rubbish was left over, she'd bring home for me. And I was really like Cinders. And there's-- there was a whole correspondence going on, which I got my records. You see, the CBF, who were the Refugee Committee originally, they wrote up records on all the children until after the war. The records ended then when they got married or they grew up. And we can get our records back now. And when my parents came-- I'm already jumping ahead. When my parents came, we decided not to tell them about it, the three of us. They had been five years in immigration-- took them five years to get to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard from them during the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We heard at first through the Red Cross messages after war started. So we knew they had got out. And we heard from Yugoslavia. We heard from Italy. Then we didn't hear anything for a  year or two. And-- but we always lived in hope because they had escaped. Then they got to Portugal. They stayed a whole year in Portugal. It took them a year to process their papers to get them to England, which was a miracle in itself. Eleanor Rathbone, the late Eleanor Rathbone, MP, she pulled-- she put a legislation through Parliament, letting people, or children under 16, if they have close family in neutral countries-- I mean, you can count them on the fingers of two hands the people who, who it, you know, it was good for-- my parents came into that category. They were allowed to come into England, which they did at the end of 1943, 1943, at the very end. That was before war-- before the war finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that they were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could correspond with them in Portugal already. That was marvelous, when we managed to get stamps, you know? I used to-- that's the one thing I spent whatever money I managed to get, I spent on stamps. And we agreed, the three of us agreed, not to mention anything to our parents that we had it so bad. Of course, they came loaded with presents for them and for us. And it took just one night for my mother, who slept in my bed-- they made them very welcome. You see, what had happened, Auntie Vera's mother ran boarding houses in Coventry. That's how we got blitzed near the Coventry Cathedral. And they went to evacuate. They had a little room in a cottage during The Blitz. But those people-- it wasn't their fault. It was the people's fault. They said they don't want any so-and-so Germans-- they called us the Germans-- in their cottage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- we begged to be left behind in the air raid shelter that was still in Coventry. But they were ashamed of the neighbors. I can understand it. And they wanted to go and evacuate themselves every night into the safety of that cottage. So they sent us, three children. We all left the house together. And we walked into the center of Coventry-- can you imagine-- into her mother's apartment house, that she had boarding houses. And there, we got blitzed out of both. She had two boarding houses. And after that, they evacuated to Yorkshire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- that is how we came to be in Yorkshire with, with them. I wish to God they'd left us behind in Coventry. But they took us with. Whatever possessed them to have the three of us, I don't know. I still can't fathom it out. And that was a wonderful thing in a way. But my brother went to work. They did pretty well. They got paid from the Refugee Committee. Not a fortune, but they did get paid. I did all the work. I went out to work. She kept all that money as well, which wasn't inconsiderable. My brother went out to work when he was 14. He worked in a factory. And, And Inge went to school. So that's how we lived until my parents came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe what it was like to be reunited with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's indescribable. We-- but we were frightened at the same time. You see, what she did-- her mother-- they thought they had another lot of, of youngsters like us on their hands. But they didn't count with my parents who'd been through five years of immigration. They weren't going to do what we did. I was a coward. I was cowed. I, I had the responsibility of my brother and sister. I was still very, very young emotionally. I was terrified. And her mother had bought a boarding house already. They were going dirt cheap in Coventry, because nobody wanted to live in Coventry. It was wartime.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she'd bought another house in-- already to install my parents as housekeepers. so they could earn their keep there. That was already sorted out months before that my parents came. So they gave the permission for my parents to come, you see, although it was the Coventry Cathedral which sponsored my sister and which sponsored us. So they got on the wrong-- they got the wrong caliber of people. They weren't going to work in a-- in somebody's boarding house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the night-- I don't think-- that was the only time in my life I ever fainted. I've been through tragedies, terrible tragedies, in my life. I lost my only son at the age of 21. I didn't faint. I had to look after my family. And I had to take it and carry on living. But that time, when my parents came, there was such a god almighty row. It was so tremendous when my parents found out what had happened to their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't find out the real thing, you know, the, the terror I lived in from Uncle Billy. That they never found out. He would have killed him. My father would have killed him, although he never succeeded. But they found out how we lived, how, how, how terrified we were, how hungry we  were, and the work that went on day and night out in the, in the factory for my brother, and for me, the housework and the factory and the cotton mill job. There was such a row. And my parents couldn't speak English. And my father, without speaking any English, went to the village-- it was a village-- to the telephone. And he summoned the Refugee Committee. How he did it, I don't know. He got through to Manchester to the Refugee Committee. And he said, if you don't come immediately and take all of us away, there's going to be the biggest ructions you've ever seen. The whole village will get to know about it. And they came. And they took us all away. And they reopened what was called [? Kircher ?] House. It wasn't that far from Manchester, about an hour and a half drive. And that was within a day. While that row was going on, I fainted. I was just 21. It was my 21st birthday that, you know, that week, really. And my parents came after. They arrived in the beginning of January. But they were segregated. They weren't allowed to come to us by the British government because of fear of spies from abroad. So they segregated my father and my mother. And they asked them questions. That took three weeks. And my mother went on hunger strike. She said she's come to see her children. And the doctor said he's not going to be responsible. He's got to release her. She's got to see her children. For three weeks, we didn't hear. They were in complete segregation, in, in isolation to be questioned by the British-- by the government. So anyway, they came a few days after my 21st birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember. It was tremendous. It was tremendous. Actually, they weren't even there, Uncle and Auntie. They were in Coventry with her mother on holiday. And like a fool, we-- as soon we heard that they were coming, we wrote and told them. So we had one day alone with our parents. And that's when my mother got all the stories-- well, most of the stories out of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to see them again after that length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bonded immediately. My brother took a bit longer. My sister took even longer. And that is one of the things that came over very strong 50 years later, the stories of the bonding with the lucky ones with their families again. Some never bonded at all. With me, it was instantaneously. It's wonderful to, you know, to have your parents back. And we, we rescued. They wouldn't leave us alone for a minute. I remember the morning, the story in the morning. That is a gem. I, I was so exhausted by all-- they, they'd returned straight away from Coventry-- I was so exhausted after that. My mother kept me talking all night. I couldn't get up in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from their bedroom comes the voice-- where's our cup of tea? That was my first job, to make a cup of tea and bring it to their room. So my mother gets out, up out of bed. And she goes into their room.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, no more cup of tea. You make cup of tea. They looked-- they'd never heard such a-- then they were-- they, they, they're thunderstruck. Then she fainted after the row.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, she was a semi-invalid, you see? That was really why. Also, I  was terrified in case she'd die on me if I started a row. You know, sometimes it boils over, the, the, the frustration in a, in a young person. And so she fainted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then her mother came running from Coventry and took her away. That was another-- you know, sort of, it two days, the whole thing. The whole neighborhood knew. My mother was screaming the place down. She was hysterical. And that's when my father went down to the village. And he called the-- And my sister didn't know about all that. She was sent to school. And a teacher in Oldham, Jewish teacher in her school, took her home. And she went to a hostel-- there was a-- in, in Manchester. She was, for a few weeks, not with my parents the first few weeks. That's why the bonding also took longer. She was kept there until-- almost until Passover. Because she was in a grammar school. And they didn't want to release her. We had to find a place in another grammar school in Birmingham. We went to Birmingham because we had cousins there. And you know, they got to know that my parents came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was now in 1940-- beginning of 1944, in January. So immediately were made plans, after the Refugee Committee took us to Manchester, where we could see my sister. And, and we lived there, in that what used to be a hostel, which they reopened specially for us. After that, we went straight to Birmingham. And there, we found a flat. It was very difficult. You know, people donated furniture and all sorts of things. Because you know, we came without, without anything. And that's when we started to become a family again, about Passover, 1944. So it was something, something to be experienced. But my sister never experienced that because she was away at school. Yeah, it was--
